attributes.stem:
A 17-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-hour history of marked palpitations. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. There is no family history of sudden cardiac death or inherited cardiac conditions.

On examination, he appears well with pulse rate 90/min, blood pressure 120/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Cardiac and respiratory examinations are unremarkable. An electrocardiogram reveals a prolonged QT interval and broad-based T waves. Blood tests are unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management in addition to lifestyle modification and monitoring?
options: Insertion of an implantable cardioverter defibrillator
attributes.explanation:
This scenario describes a young person with a likely diagnosis of congenital long QT syndrome, suggested by the electrocardiogram findings of a prolonged QT interval and broad-based T waves, in the absence of secondary causes or a family history of sudden cardiac death. Congenital long QT syndrome is an inherited channelopathy that increases the risk of ventricular arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death, but risk stratification is based on symptoms and prior events.

Oral propranolol, in tandem with lifestyle modification, is the most appropriate initial management for a person with congenital long QT syndrome. Propranolol and nadolol are the beta-blockers that have demonstrated efficacy in treating congenital long QT syndrome. Beta-blocker therapy is recommended for all patients with congenital long QT syndrome, regardless of whether they have experienced symptoms, as it reduces the risk of syncope and sudden cardiac death. Lifestyle modification includes avoiding strenuous exercise, sudden loud noises, and medications that prolong the QT interval. Ongoing monitoring is important to assess for symptom recurrence or risk escalation.

Insertion of an implantable cardioverter defibrillator would be indicated for people with long QT syndrome who have survived a cardiac arrest, have recurrent syncope despite beta-blocker therapy, or are at very high risk. In this scenario, the absence of prior cardiac events means an implantable cardioverter defibrillator is not warranted at this stage.

Amiodarone is an antiarrhythmic medication that can actually prolong the QT interval and potentially worsen the condition in patients with congenital long QT syndrome. It would be contraindicated in this patient as it could increase the risk of torsades de pointes and sudden cardiac death.

Stellate ganglionectomy is a surgical intervention reserved for people with long QT syndrome who continue to have life-threatening arrhythmias despite optimal medical therapy and an implantable cardioverter defibrillator, which is not indicated in this low-risk presentation.

Cardiac ablation is used to treat specific arrhythmias by destroying small areas of cardiac tissue that are causing abnormal electrical signals. It is not a standard treatment for congenital long QT syndrome, which is a disorder of myocardial repolarisation rather than an abnormal conduction pathway that can be targeted with ablation.
learningPoints: Propranolol and nadolol are the only beta-blockers with evidence of benefit for treating congenital long QT syndrome.
Beta-blocker therapy should be given to both symptomatic and asymptomatic patients with congenital long QT syndrome to reduce the risk of syncope and sudden cardiac death.
Implantable cardioverter defibrillator is reserved for congenital long QT syndrome patients with prior cardiac arrest or ongoing symptoms despite medical therapy.
references: Wilde AAM, Amin AS, Postema PG. Diagnosis, management and therapeutic strategies for congenital long QT syndrome. Heart. 2022;108(5):332-338. doi:10.1136/heartjnl-2020-318259

Hauwanga WN, Yau RCC, Goh KS, Castro Ceron JI, Alphonse B, Singh G, Elamin S, Jamched V, Abraham AA, Purvil J, Devan JN, Valentim G, McBenedict B, Lima Pessôa B, Mesquita ET. Management of long QT syndrome: a systematic review. Cureus. 2024 Jun 18;16(6):e62592. doi: 10.7759/cureus.62592.
Torsades de pointes and congenital long QT syndrome [published 2023 June]. In: Therapeutic Guidelines. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited; accessed {16 September 2025}. https://www.tg.org.au
